items.q: Does the social model say biology is irrelevant to health?
items.correct: No - it argues social factors also strongly shape health, alongside biology.
items.misconception: The social model ignores the body entirely.
items.random_wrong: It claims only the rich ever get ill.
items.why: The social model adds social causes to biological ones; it does not deny that biology matters.

items.correct: Treating non-medical or everyday conditions as medical problems.
items.misconception: It simply means curing more diseases.
items.why: Medicalisation is about expanding what counts as 'medical', such as defining shyness as a disorder.

items.q: Why do sociologists say illness is socially constructed?
items.correct: Because what counts as illness varies across cultures and over time.
items.misconception: It means illnesses are not real.
items.random_wrong: It means everyone defines health in the same way.
items.why: Social construction is about changing definitions, not denying that people genuinely suffer.

items.q: The biomedical model defines health as:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The absence of biological disease. “Social wellbeing” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Parsons' 'sick role' suggests an ill person is:
items.correct: Temporarily exempt from duties but expected to seek help
items.why: It's Temporarily exempt from duties but expected to seek help. “Permanently excluded from society” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Redefining social conditions as medical problems
items.why: It's Redefining social conditions as medical problems. “Curing all diseases” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Health inequalities are differences in health linked mainly to:
items.correct: Social factors such as class and poverty
items.why: It's Social factors such as class and poverty. “The weather” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
